questions.question: What is the main purpose of the Temporal Developer Skill for AI coding agents?

choices.text: It gives an AI coding agent expert knowledge of Temporal's programming model, including workflow determinism, activity patterns, retry policies, and common pitfalls.
It connects an AI coding agent to a live Temporal cluster so it can execute workflows and activities on your behalf during development.
It automatically generates Temporal workflow code from plain English descriptions and deploys it to Temporal Cloud without any manual steps.
It monitors a running Temporal Worker and alerts the AI coding agent whenever a workflow fails or an activity exceeds its retry limit.

questions.explanation: The Temporal Developer Skill embeds domain-specific Temporal expertise directly into an AI coding agent. It covers the programming model — things like workflow determinism rules, activity patterns, retry policies, error handling, testing strategies, and versioning — so the agent can give accurate, context-aware guidance while you write code. It does not run workflows, generate and deploy code automatically, or monitor live clusters.

questions.question: Why does the Temporal Docs MCP Server require authentication with a Google account?

choices.text: To verify that the developer has an active Temporal Cloud subscription before granting access to the documentation.
To enforce rate limits and prevent abuse, while not collecting any personal contact information from the user.
To link documentation queries to a specific Temporal namespace so answers are customized to the user's cluster configuration.
To create a personalized learning profile that tracks which Temporal concepts the developer has already studied.

questions.explanation: The MCP server uses anonymous Google account authentication purely as a lightweight mechanism to enforce rate limits and prevent abuse. The documentation explicitly states that Temporal cannot see and does not collect any contact information from this process. There is no subscription check, namespace linkage, or learning profile involved.

questions.question: What key advantage does the Temporal Docs MCP Server provide over relying on an AI assistant's built-in training data alone?

choices.text: It compiles and runs Temporal SDK code locally so the AI assistant can verify its suggestions before showing them to the developer.
It caches a one-time snapshot of Temporal documentation taken at the assistant's training cutoff date for faster lookups.
It gives the AI assistant real-time access to current Temporal documentation, so answers reflect up-to-date information rather than potentially outdated training data.
It translates Temporal documentation into the programming language the developer is using, so examples always match the chosen SDK.

questions.explanation: AI models have a training data cutoff, meaning their built-in knowledge can become stale as Temporal evolves. The Temporal Docs MCP Server solves this by giving the AI assistant a live connection to current Temporal documentation at query time. This means the assistant can draw on the latest information rather than relying solely on what it learned during training, which is especially valuable for a fast-moving platform like Temporal.

questions.question: A developer wants their AI coding agent to help debug a Temporal Cloud connectivity issue, including TLS certificate problems and namespace authentication errors. Which Temporal AI tool is most appropriate for this use case?

choices.text: The Temporal Developer Skill, because it covers all Temporal topics including Cloud connectivity and authentication configuration.
The Temporal Docs MCP Server, because it provides real-time access to all Temporal documentation including Cloud troubleshooting guides.
The Temporal Cloud Skill, because it is specifically designed to help AI agents troubleshoot Temporal Cloud connectivity, authentication, and configuration issues.
Both the Temporal Developer Skill and the Temporal Cloud Skill together, since neither covers Cloud connectivity on its own.

questions.explanation: The Temporal Cloud Skill (github.com/temporalio/skill-temporal-cloud) is purpose-built for Temporal Cloud connectivity, authentication, and configuration troubleshooting — exactly what the developer needs. The Temporal Developer Skill focuses on the programming model (determinism, activities, retry policies, testing, etc.) and does not specialize in Cloud-specific operational concerns. The MCP Server gives real-time doc access but is a documentation retrieval tool, not a specialized skill. Using both skills simultaneously is unnecessary when one is already scoped to the problem.

questions.question: What is the key functional difference between the Temporal Developer Skill and the Temporal Docs MCP Server?

choices.text: The Temporal Developer Skill only works with Claude Code, while the MCP Server works with any MCP-compatible tool including Cursor and Codex.

The Temporal Developer Skill embeds domain-specific Temporal expertise into the agent (determinism rules, patterns, gotchas), while the MCP Server gives the agent real-time retrieval access to current Temporal documentation.
The Temporal Developer Skill requires a paid Temporal Cloud account, while the MCP Server uses anonymous Google authentication and is free to use.
The Temporal Developer Skill is installed once globally for all projects, while the MCP Server must be re-configured per project because it cannot be scoped globally.

questions.explanation: The core distinction is the nature of the knowledge. Skills bake domain expertise directly into the agent — covering workflow determinism, activity patterns, retry policies, testing strategies, versioning, and common gotchas — so the agent reasons from that embedded knowledge. The MCP Server is a retrieval mechanism: it connects the agent to live Temporal documentation so answers draw from current docs rather than potentially stale training data. Both are useful but serve different purposes. Option A is wrong — the Developer Skill supports Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, and any agent supporting Skills (via npx or manual install). Option C is incorrect — neither tool requires a paid Cloud account; the MCP Server uses anonymous Google auth for rate limiting only. Option D is wrong — the MCP Server explicitly supports global scoping with `--scope user` in Claude Code.

questions.question: A developer uses Cursor as their primary coding agent and wants to install the Temporal Developer Skill. Which of the following is a valid installation method?

choices.text: Run `claude mcp add --scope user --transport http temporal-docs https://temporal.mcp.kapa.ai` inside Cursor's terminal.
Run `/plugin marketplace add temporalio/claude-temporal-plugin` inside Cursor's agent chat to add the marketplace, then install the skill.
Install from the Cursor Marketplace directly, or run `/add-plugin temporal` inside Cursor's agent chat.
Clone the skill repository with `git clone https://github.com/temporalio/skill-temporal-developer.git` and place it in the Cursor extensions directory.

questions.explanation: The documentation explicitly lists two options for Cursor: installing from the Cursor Marketplace at cursor.com/marketplace/temporal, or running `/add-plugin temporal` in Cursor's agent chat. Option A describes the MCP Server setup command for Claude Code, not a Skill installation for Cursor. Option B describes the Claude Code plugin marketplace flow, which is specific to Claude Code and not applicable to Cursor. Option D partially describes the manual installation approach, but the manual clone method targets `~/.claude/skills/` (Claude's skills directory), not a Cursor extensions directory — the docs note you should change the target directory for non-Claude agents, but this is not how Cursor is documented to be set up.

questions.question: A team wants to add the Temporal Docs MCP Server to a single project so that only that project's Claude Code sessions have access to it. How should they configure this?

choices.text: Run `claude mcp add --scope user --transport http temporal-docs https://temporal.mcp.kapa.ai`, which stores the configuration in the user profile and limits it to the current project's sessions.
Run `claude mcp add --transport http temporal-docs https://temporal.mcp.kapa.ai` without the `--scope user` flag, which stores the configuration in the project's `.mcp.json` file.
Add the MCP server URL to the project's `package.json` under a `temporalMcp` key, which Claude Code reads automatically on project startup.
Configure the server in Claude Desktop under Settings > Connectors, then link that connector to the specific project directory in the Claude Code workspace settings.

questions.explanation: The documentation is explicit: to add the MCP server to a specific project only, omit the `--scope user` flag. This stores the server configuration in the project's `.mcp.json` file, scoping it to that project. Option A is wrong — including `--scope user` makes the server globally available across all projects, the opposite of what's needed. Option C is fabricated; Claude Code does not read MCP server configuration from `package.json`. Option D describes a Claude Desktop flow, which is a different application from Claude Code and does not support project-level scoping through that UI.

questions.question: Your team is using an AI coding agent (Claude Code) to scaffold new Temporal workflows. A developer notices the agent keeps generating non-deterministic workflow code — using random UUIDs directly inside workflow functions, calling time.Now() without the workflow clock API, and spawning goroutines outside workflow context. Which tooling change would most directly address the root cause of these mistakes?

choices.text: Add the Temporal Docs MCP Server so the agent can query up-to-date API reference pages when generating code, replacing stale training-data knowledge with current documentation.

Install the Temporal Developer Skill, which encodes workflow determinism rules, activity patterns, and common gotchas directly into the agent's domain expertise rather than relying on general training data.
Switch to a different SDK language, since some SDKs enforce determinism at compile time and would cause the agent to generate errors rather than silently wrong code.
Run temporal workflow show on generated workflows to detect non-determinism errors at runtime, then feed those error logs back to the agent as correction prompts.

questions.explanation: The Temporal Developer Skill is specifically designed to give AI coding agents 'expert-level knowledge of Temporal's programming model — workflow determinism rules, activity patterns, retry policies, error handling, testing strategies, worker configuration, versioning, and common gotchas.' The mistakes described (random UUIDs in workflow, time.Now(), unmanaged goroutines) are all classic determinism violations. The MCP Server (A) provides real-time doc lookup but doesn't embed determinism rules as persistent expertise — it helps when the agent actively queries docs, not when generating code from instinct. Switching SDKs (C) is not a real solution; all SDKs can be misused. The feedback loop approach (D) is reactive and expensive — it requires broken deployments to generate corrections. The Skill proactively prevents these errors by teaching the agent the programming model.

questions.question: A platform team connects the Temporal Docs MCP Server to their Claude Desktop instance to help debug Temporal Cloud connectivity issues. They notice the agent sometimes gives accurate answers about mTLS certificate rotation, but other times falls back to outdated responses that contradict the current docs. What is the most likely operational reason for this inconsistency, and how should it be addressed?

choices.text: The MCP server caches responses per session and does not re-fetch docs mid-conversation; the team should start a new Claude session for each distinct topic to force a fresh doc fetch.

The MCP server requires per-request authentication; if the Google account token expires mid-session, subsequent queries silently fall back to the model's static training data instead of returning an error.

The Temporal Docs MCP Server only covers the Temporal Developer Skill topics (workflow determinism, retry policies); Temporal Cloud connectivity issues require the separate Temporal Cloud Skill instead.

The agent may not be invoking the MCP server for every response — it may use training data when it 'feels confident' about a topic. For Cloud-specific issues, the Temporal Cloud Skill provides embedded, persistent expertise rather than on-demand doc retrieval.

questions.explanation: MCP servers provide real-time documentation access, but the AI agent decides when to query them. For topics where the model has strong (possibly outdated) training-data confidence — like mTLS certificate handling — it may not invoke the MCP server at all, producing inconsistent results. The Temporal Cloud Skill ('helps your AI coding agent troubleshoot Temporal Cloud connectivity, authentication, and configuration issues') embeds that expertise persistently, so the agent applies it reliably rather than only when it chooses to query docs. Option B is plausible-sounding but authentication with the MCP server uses anonymous Google auth for rate limiting — it doesn't silently degrade to training data on token expiry. Option C is wrong; the MCP server covers all Temporal docs, not just developer topics. Option A is not how MCP servers or Claude sessions are documented to work.

questions.question: A developer wants to add the Temporal Docs MCP Server to a shared team repository so every contributor's Claude Code instance automatically has access to current Temporal docs, without requiring each person to run a global setup command. Which configuration approach achieves this?

choices.text: Run 'claude mcp add --scope user --transport http temporal-docs https://temporal.mcp.kapa.ai' on each developer's machine; the --scope user flag registers it globally so it persists across all their projects.

Run 'claude mcp add --transport http temporal-docs https://temporal.mcp.kapa.ai' (without --scope user) in the repository root; this stores the server config in the project's .mcp.json file, which can be committed and shared.
Add the MCP server URL to the repository's package.json or build config so it is picked up automatically by Claude Code when the project is opened, without needing any explicit registration command.
Use the npx skills CLI to install the skill into ~/.claude/skills/temporal-docs; skills stored in that directory are automatically shared across all projects cloned on the same machine.

questions.explanation: The docs explicitly state: 'To add the server to a specific project only, omit the --scope user flag. This stores the configuration in the project's .mcp.json file.' A .mcp.json file in the repo root is committed to version control, so every team member who clones the repository gets the MCP server configuration automatically — no per-developer setup needed. Option A (--scope user) does the opposite: it registers globally for one user's machine, not per-project for the whole team. Option C (package.json) is not a mechanism Temporal or Claude Code supports for MCP registration. Option D conflates Skills (domain expertise bundles) with the MCP Server (real-time doc retrieval); Skills are installed into ~/.claude/skills but are not the same as MCP server registration, and they don't automatically become project-scoped.

questions.question: An engineering team uses Cursor for development and wants AI-assisted help with both writing correct Temporal workflow code AND troubleshooting Temporal Cloud authentication errors (e.g., mTLS cert rejections, API key misconfiguration). They install only the Temporal Developer Skill via the Cursor Marketplace. A week later they report the agent is great at workflow determinism but still gives wrong advice on Cloud connectivity. What is the gap, and what is the correct fix?

choices.text: The Temporal Developer Skill covers all Temporal topics including Cloud; the real issue is that the skill wasn't restarted after install. Restarting Cursor will reload the skill and fix the Cloud connectivity advice.
The Cursor Marketplace skill only installs a subset of Temporal knowledge; to get full coverage the team should also add the Temporal Docs MCP Server, which covers Cloud topics the skill omits.

The two Skills have distinct scopes: the Temporal Developer Skill covers the programming model, while the Temporal Cloud Skill specifically handles connectivity, authentication, and configuration. Both need to be installed.

Skills don't support Cursor for Cloud topics; for Temporal Cloud troubleshooting in Cursor the team must use the MCP server approach exclusively, as Skills only work with Claude Code and Codex for that domain.

questions.explanation: The documentation describes two separate Skills with distinct scopes: the Temporal Developer Skill provides 'expert-level knowledge of Temporal's programming model — workflow determinism rules, activity patterns, retry policies...' while the Temporal Cloud Skill 'helps your AI coding agent troubleshoot Temporal Cloud connectivity, authentication, and configuration issues.' Installing only one leaves a gap in the other domain. This is a deliberate separation of concerns in the tooling design. Option A is wrong — restarting is required after install but doesn't expand the skill's knowledge scope. Option B is plausible but inaccurate; the MCP Server provides real-time doc access (which is complementary) but the correct structured fix for persistent Cloud expertise is the dedicated Cloud Skill. Option D is wrong; Skills work with any agent that supports the Skills protocol, and both Skills are installable via npx which works with Cursor-compatible agents.

questions.question: An engineering team is building a Temporal-based order processing system and decides to use an AI coding agent (Claude Code with the Temporal Developer Skill installed) to accelerate development. The agent generates a workflow that uses `workflow.Sleep` inside a goroutine spawned with `workflow.Go`, with the goroutine closing over a channel that is also used by the main workflow coroutine. During load testing, the team observes non-determinism panics on replay. The AI agent is asked to debug the issue, but it keeps suggesting fixes based on general Go concurrency patterns (mutexes, sync.WaitGroup) rather than Temporal-specific coroutine scheduling. Which root cause best explains BOTH the non-determinism AND the agent's incorrect suggestions?

choices.text: The Temporal Developer Skill only covers activity patterns and retry policies, so it lacks knowledge of workflow coroutine scheduling rules; the agent defaults to standard Go concurrency advice because the skill's knowledge boundary ends at the activity layer.

The non-determinism arises because standard Go sync primitives (mutexes, WaitGroup) block the underlying OS thread rather than yielding to the Temporal coroutine scheduler, corrupting event ordering on replay; the agent errs because its base training data associates these patterns with correct concurrent Go, and the skill must be restarted or re-queried with explicit Temporal context to override that prior.

The non-determinism is caused by the MCP server returning stale documentation that predates Temporal's coroutine model, so the agent receives outdated workflow determinism rules and recommends standard Go primitives that were valid in an earlier SDK version.

Closing over a channel across workflow coroutines is unsupported by the SDK's dispatcher and always causes a panic regardless of sleep placement; the agent's suggestion of mutexes is architecturally correct but requires wrapping in workflow.ExecuteActivity to be safe.

questions.explanation:
Temporal's Go SDK replaces the normal Go scheduler with a deterministic coroutine dispatcher. All workflow coroutines (spawned via workflow.Go) must yield only through Temporal-aware blocking calls (workflow.Sleep, workflow.Channel.Receive, etc.). Standard sync primitives like sync.Mutex or sync.WaitGroup block the real OS thread, bypassing the coroutine scheduler entirely. On replay, the event order driven by the history may differ from the original execution's goroutine interleaving, causing non-determinism panics. The underlying design principle is that workflow code must be a pure function of the history: only the Temporal dispatcher controls which coroutine runs when.

The agent's incorrect advice stems from a well-known limitation of LLM-based tooling: base training data heavily weights standard Go concurrency idioms, which are correct outside Temporal but catastrophic inside a workflow. The Temporal Developer Skill provides domain-specific priors about workflow determinism rules, but if the engineer doesn't frame the question in Temporal terms (e.g., asks 'how do I fix a data race' rather than 'why does my workflow replay panic'), the skill may not override the model's strong general Go priors. The fix is to use workflow.Channel and workflow.Go exclusively, never crossing into stdlib sync. This scenario teaches two lessons simultaneously: (1) workflow coroutine scheduling is not Go goroutine scheduling, and (2) AI skills augment but do not fully replace precise, domain-framed prompting — the engineer must understand enough Temporal internals to ask the right question.
